title: Geometry
objective: Students will explore geometry concepts including 3-D shapes (age 5+), Turns & Directions, Building & Drawing Shapes and more.

steps.content: Today we're going to learn about 3-D shapes (age 5+). Get ready for an adventure!
Analyse and compare 2-D and 3-D shapes using informal language to describe sides, vertices, and other attributes
Let's practice together! Count the sides and corners of a shape
Can you describe what makes two shapes different — for example, explaining that a triangle has 3 corners and 3 sides while a square has 4 of each?
You did it! Here's what we explored about 3-D shapes (age 5+): Count the sides and corners of a shape Compare a triangle and a rectangle by number of sides
Let's explore Turns & Directions! Have you ever wondered about this?
Describe movement and direction, including whole, half, quarter, and three-quarter turns
Let me show you something cool. Follow instructions to move forward, backward, turn left/right
If you is playing a game where they have to turn left or right, do they understand what a quarter turn, half turn, and full turn mean?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Turns & Directions. Follow instructions to move forward, backward, turn left/right Demonstrate a whole turn, half turn, and quarter turn with their body
Let's discover the amazing world of Building & Drawing Shapes!
Model shapes by building from components (e.g. sticks and clay balls) and by drawing
Time to try this out: Build a triangle from three sticks and three clay balls
Can you build or draw a shape they've been shown — for example, making a cube from folded paper or copying a triangle onto dot paper?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Building & Drawing Shapes. Build a triangle from three sticks and three clay balls Draw a recognisable rectangle
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Combining Simple Shapes!
Compose simple shapes to form larger shapes (e.g. two triangles make a rectangle)
Time to try this out: Join two triangles to make a rectangle or larger triangle
Can you fit smaller shapes together to make bigger ones — like combining two triangles to make a rectangle?
Great job! Today we learned about Combining Simple Shapes. Join two triangles to make a rectangle or larger triangle Use pattern blocks to fill a hexagon outline
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Flat vs Solid Shapes!
Distinguish two-dimensional (flat) shapes from three-dimensional (solid) shapes
Let's practice together! Sort shapes into 'flat' and 'solid' groups
Does you understand the difference between a flat shape like a square and a solid shape like a cube — and can they sort everyday objects into flat or solid?
Great job! Today we learned about Flat vs Solid Shapes. Sort shapes into 'flat' and 'solid' groups Explain that a circle is flat but a sphere is solid
